lesson.title: Diagonalization
lesson.body: A matrix A is diagonalizable if A = PDP⁻¹ where D is diagonal (containing eigenvalues) and P has eigenvectors as columns. A is diagonalizable if and only if it has n linearly independent eigenvectors. Once diagonalized, computing matrix powers is easy: Aⁿ = PDⁿP⁻¹, where Dⁿ just raises each diagonal entry to the n-th power.

rooms.question: In A = PDP⁻¹, the diagonal entries of D are:
rooms.explanation: D is the diagonal matrix of eigenvalues, and P is the matrix whose columns are the corresponding eigenvectors.

rooms.question: A n×n matrix is definitely diagonalizable if it has:
rooms.explanation: n distinct eigenvalues guarantee n linearly independent eigenvectors, ensuring diagonalizability. The converse is not always true.

rooms.question: If A = PDP⁻¹, what is A⁵?
steps: D^5 \text{ just raises each diagonal entry to the 5th power}
rooms.explanation: Powers of a diagonalized matrix: Aⁿ = PDⁿP⁻¹. This is the main reason to diagonalize — easy computation of powers.

rooms.question: Order the steps to diagonalize matrix A:
items: Find all eigenvalues from det(A − λI) = 0
Form P (columns = eigenvectors) and D (diagonal = eigenvalues)
rooms.explanation: Eigenvalues first, then eigenvectors, then assemble P and D, then verify.

rooms.question: When is a matrix NOT diagonalizable?
answers: When it does not have n linearly independent eigenvectors
rooms.explanation: A defective matrix has fewer linearly independent eigenvectors than its size (e.g., [[1,1],[0,1]] has only one independent eigenvector for λ=1).

boss.name: The Diagonal Demon
boss.taunt: I reduce all complexity to a diagonal. Nothing can hide in my basis.

questions.question: If A = PDP⁻¹ and D has eigenvalues 2 and 3, what is det(A)?
questions.explanation: det(A) = det(PDP⁻¹) = det(D) = product of eigenvalues = 2·3 = 6.

questions.question: The Spectral Theorem states that every real symmetric matrix A is:
answers: Orthogonally diagonalizable: A = QDQᵀ where Q is orthogonal
questions.explanation: Spectral Theorem: symmetric A = QDQᵀ with Q orthogonal (Q⁻¹ = Qᵀ) and D real diagonal. Eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal.

questions.question: If A = PDP⁻¹ and all eigenvalues |λᵢ| < 1, then Aⁿ as n→∞ approaches:
questions.explanation: Aⁿ = PDⁿP⁻¹. If all |λᵢ| < 1, then Dⁿ → 0, so Aⁿ → PDP⁻¹·0 = 0. This has applications in Markov chains and stability analysis.
